name: Free List

x.invariant: every block is either handed out to a caller or linked into the free list; no block is in both states.
operations: allocate (O(1): pop a block / O(n) on first-fit search for variable sizes)
optional coalescing of adjacent free blocks
x.memoryOverhead: The free pointer is stored inside free blocks themselves (intrusive), so near-zero metadata while allocated.
x.locality: Reused blocks may be cold/scattered; LIFO reuse tends to be warmer than FIFO.
x.mutationModel: Mutable pool; freed blocks are recycled rather than returned to the OS.
x.concurrency: Not thread-safe by default; per-thread free lists or lock-free stacks avoid contention.

def: A linked list of reusable memory blocks that an allocator pops to allocate and pushes to free, giving O(1) fixed-size allocation without per-block OS calls.
problem: Calling the OS / general allocator for every small same-sized object is slow and fragmenting.
mechanism: Pre-allocates a pool and threads free blocks into an intrusive singly-linked list (the next pointer lives in the free block). Allocation pops the head; freeing pushes it back. Variable-size variants search (first/best-fit) and coalesce neighbours.
limits: Fixed-size pools waste space for mixed sizes
Memory not returned to the OS
failures: Use-after-free / double-free corrupt the intrusive links
ABA in naive lock-free free lists
whenNot: Highly variable allocation sizes (use a general allocator / arena)
When freed memory must return to the OS promptly
myths: "Free lists eliminate fragmentation" — fixed-size ones do; variable-size ones still fragment
